title: Python print() and variables
summary: Learn how Python shows text on the screen and stores values in variables.
goal: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to store simple values in variables and display them with print().
explanation: A variable is a named box that stores a value. Instead of repeating the same text or number again and again, you can save it once and reuse it.
The print() function tells Python to show something in the output area. This is often the first tool beginners use to check whether their code is working.
When you combine variables with print(), your programs become easier to read and easier to update later.
code:
# Store a name in a variable
student_name = 'Alya'

# Store a number in another variable
completed_lessons = 3

# print() shows text on the screen
print('Welcome to Coding Hub!')

# We can also print the values stored in variables
print(student_name)
print(completed_lessons)

# This message combines words and variable values
print(student_name, 'has finished', completed_lessons, 'lessons.')
codeNotes: student_name stores text, while completed_lessons stores a number.
print('Welcome to Coding Hub!') shows a simple fixed message.
print(student_name) uses the value inside the variable instead of printing the word student_name itself.
The final print() joins several pieces together so the output reads like a sentence.
commonMistakes: Writing print without parentheses, like print 'Hello', which does not work in modern Python.
Using a variable name before giving it a value first.

exercise.title: Make your own welcome message
exercise.instructions: Create a variable for your name and a variable for your favorite coding language. Then print a sentence that says you are starting that language on Coding Hub.

title: Python if statements
summary: Learn how Python makes decisions by checking whether a condition is true.
goal: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to write a basic if statement that runs code only when a condition is true.
explanation: Programs often need to decide what to do next. An if statement lets Python choose based on a condition.
A condition is a question that returns either True or False. If it is True, the indented code below the if statement runs.
Indentation matters in Python. The indented lines belong to the if block.
code:
score = 85

# Check whether the score is high enough
if score >= 70:
    # This line only runs when the condition is true
    print('You passed the lesson!')

print('The program has finished.')
codeNotes: score >= 70 asks whether score is greater than or equal to 70.
Because 85 is greater than 70, Python runs the indented print() line.
The final print() is not indented, so it runs no matter what.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the colon : at the end of the if line.
Not indenting the code inside the if block.
Using = instead of == when trying to compare values.

exercise.title: Check a learner level
exercise.instructions: Create a variable called lessons_finished. If the value is 5 or more, print a message that says the learner can unlock the next stage.
